title: What This Data Is (and Isn't)
description: Before analyzing anything, understand what the Minnesota felony sentencing dataset actually represents: where it sits in the criminal-justice funnel, what one row means, and which questions it can and cannot answer.
author: unmanaged
objectives: Explain what administrative data is and how it differs from data collected for research
Locate felony sentencing data within the criminal-justice funnel and the dark figure of crime
Apply Sellin's dictum to judge how directly a crime statistic measures crime itself
State the unit of analysis: what one row in this dataset does and does not represent
Distinguish a descriptive pattern in the data from a causal explanation of it

steps.title: Before you analyze, understand
steps.body:
Every other module in this course teaches you to *do* something with the data, you'll filter it, summarize it, compare groups. This module asks a prior question: **what is this data, and what can it honestly tell you?**

That question is not a formality. Numbers about crime are among the most misread statistics in public life. In 23 of 27 national surveys taken since 1993, most Americans said crime was rising, even across a long stretch when recorded crime was falling (Gramlich, 2024). A statistic is only as trustworthy as your understanding of where it came from and what it leaves out (Best, 2001).

So before you touch a filter, we will trace this dataset back to its origin: who created it, why, and at what point in the life of a crime it was recorded. By the end you should be able to say, in your own words, what questions these roughly **294,000 Minnesota felony sentencing records (2001 through 2019)** *can* answer, and, just as important, which they *cannot*.

---

**References**

Best, J. (2001). *Damned lies and statistics: Untangling numbers from the media, politicians, and activists.* University of California Press.

Gramlich, J. (2024, April 24). *What the data says about crime in the U.S.* Pew Research Center. https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2024/04/24/what-the-data-says-about-crime-in-the-us/

steps.title: Administrative data: built to run a system, not to answer your question
steps.body:
This dataset is **administrative data** which is to say information generated as a by-product of running an organization, rather than collected to answer a research question (Connelly et al., 2016). Courts and the Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission record sentences because the state must document and monitor how people are punished, not because a researcher designed a study.

That origin is a strength. Administrative records cover nearly every case, span decades, and are not subject to survey sampling error. But it is also a limit. Because the data was shaped by the needs of the system that produced it, using it for research means carefully reconstructing how each field was defined and recorded: the categories, rules, and exceptions were chosen by administrators, not by you (Connelly et al., 2016).

Throughout this course, treat every column as an *administrative decision* first and a *measurement* second. Asking "why would the system record it this way?" will save you from many misreadings.

---

**References**

Connelly, R., Playford, C. J., Gayle, V., & Dibben, C. (2016). The role of administrative data in the big data revolution in social science research. *Social Science Research, 59*, 1–12. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssresearch.2016.04.015

steps.title: Where this data comes from: the Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission
steps.body:
In 1978 the Minnesota Legislature created the **Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission**, and in 1980 Minnesota became the first state to sentence felonies under presumptive guidelines developed by such a commission (Frase, 2005; Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, n.d.). The guidelines set a **presumptive sentence** for each case from two facts: the severity of the offense and the offender's criminal-history score.

To monitor whether courts follow the guidelines, the Commission maintains a record of everyone convicted of a felony in adult court and sentenced under them, assembled from probation officers' sentencing worksheets and court data (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2024). Your dataset is a slice of that monitoring system. For scale, annual felony case volumes have ranged from about 5,500 in 1981 to a peak of 18,288 in 2017 (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2023).

This matters for interpretation. The data exists to answer the Commission's question *are sentences following the guidelines?*, the data was designed and collected to serve an administrative purpose. It was never designed to measure how much crime Minnesota experiences, or why. Keeping that original purpose in view keeps your conclusions honest.

---

**References**

Frase, R. S. (2005). Sentencing guidelines in Minnesota, 1978–2003. *Crime and Justice, 32*, 131–219. https://doi.org/10.1086/655354

Frase, R. S. (2013). *Just sentencing: Principles and procedures for a workable system.* Oxford University Press.

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (n.d.). *About MSGC.* https://mn.gov/sentencing-guidelines/about/

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2023). *2023 sentencing practices report: Summary statistics for felony cases.* https://mn.gov/sentencing-guidelines/assets/2023_MSGC_Annual_Summary_Statistics_tcm30-680133.pdf

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2024). *Report to the legislature.* https://www.lrl.mn.gov/docs/2024/mandated/240055.pdf

steps.title: The funnel: a crime's long journey to a sentence
steps.body:
A sentence is the *last* event in a long sequence. For a felony sentence to exist, a crime must have been committed, noticed, reported, recorded by police, investigated, an arrest made, charges filed, guilt established, and a felony-level sentence imposed. Criminologists picture this as a **funnel**: many events enter at the top, and only a fraction reach the bottom. The idea that criminal justice is a connected *system*, with cases dropping out at each stage, was set out by the President's Commission on Law Enforcement and Administration of Justice (1967).

Your dataset sits at the very **bottom** of that funnel. It contains only cases that survived every prior stage *and* ended in a felony sentence. It therefore says nothing directly about:

- crimes no one reported,
- reported crimes that led to no arrest,
- arrests that led to no charge or conviction,
- offenses resolved as misdemeanors or dismissed.

Each of those is a stage of the funnel your data never sees. Next we look at the widest, most invisible part of the funnel: crime that never enters at all.

---

**References**

President's Commission on Law Enforcement and Administration of Justice. (1967). *The challenge of crime in a free society.* U.S. Government Printing Office.

steps.title: The dark figure of crime
steps.body:
Criminologists call the crime that never appears in official records the **dark figure of crime**: occurrences that are called crime but are registered in no agency's statistics (Biderman & Reiss, 1967). The dark figure is not small. In the National Crime Victimization Survey, only about **42% of violent victimizations were reported to police in 2022** meaning nearly six in ten went unreported (Morgan & Thompson, 2023).

Unreported crime is also not a random sample of all crime. It skews toward less serious incidents, smaller losses, less injury (Skogan, 1977). So the crimes that *do* climb the funnel toward a felony sentence are systematically different from the crimes that don't.

For your dataset, the lesson is stark. Before a single row was recorded, most crime had already dropped out of view and dropped out *selectively*. No filter you apply can recover it.

---

**References**

Biderman, A. D., & Reiss, A. J., Jr. (1967). On exploring the 'dark figure' of crime. *The Annals of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 374*(1), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1177/000271626737400102

Morgan, R. E., & Thompson, A. (2023). *Criminal victimization, 2022* (NCJ 307089). Bureau of Justice Statistics. https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/criminal-victimization-2022

Skogan, W. G. (1977). Dimensions of the dark figure of unreported crime. *Crime & Delinquency, 23*(1), 41–50. https://doi.org/10.1177/001112877702300104

steps.title: Check: the dark figure
steps.body: The National Crime Victimization Survey finds that a large share of violent victimizations are never reported to police. What does this imply for a dataset of felony *sentences*?

options: Much crime never enters the funnel at all, so sentencing data cannot measure how much crime occurred
Sentencing data slightly overcounts crime, because every crime is eventually sentenced
Reporting rates have no effect on what appears in sentencing data

steps.title: Sellin's rule: the farther from the crime, the weaker the measure
steps.body:
In 1931 the criminologist Thorsten Sellin stated a principle now known as **Sellin's dictum**: the farther a statistic is collected from the criminal act itself measured in procedural steps, the less reliable it is as an index of crime (Sellin, 1931). Police reports sit closer to the event than arrests; arrests closer than convictions; convictions closer than sentences.

Sentences are as far down the procedural chain as it is possible to go. By Sellin's rule, that makes felony sentencing data an *excellent* record of one thing, **what the system decided to do**, and a *poor* proxy for another: **how much crime actually happened**.

This single idea reframes the entire course. Your ~294,000 records are a rich, reliable record of Minnesota's sentencing *decisions*. Read as a map of crime itself, they are the weakest measure available. Almost every mistake in interpreting this data comes from confusing the two.

---

**References**

Sellin, T. (1931). The basis of a crime index. *Journal of Criminal Law and Criminology, 22*(3), 335–356.

steps.title: Check: applying Sellin's dictum
steps.body: Using Sellin's dictum, rank these three by how *directly* they measure the amount of crime that actually occurred, from **most** direct to **least** direct: (a) a victimization survey, (b) arrest records, (c) felony sentences.

options: Victimization survey → arrests → sentences (sentences are the least direct)
Felony sentences → arrests → victimization survey (sentences are the most direct)
They are all equally direct measures of crime

steps.title: How the nation measures crime, and how Minnesota's data differs
steps.body:
Because no single source captures all crime, the United States uses two national systems that sit at *different* points in the funnel. The FBI's Uniform Crime Reporting program, now transitioning to the more detailed National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS), counts crimes *recorded by police* (Federal Bureau of Investigation, n.d.). The Bureau of Justice Statistics' National Crime Victimization Survey instead *asks a national sample of households* about crimes they experienced, including those never reported to police (Bureau of Justice Statistics, n.d.-b). The two disagree by design, because they measure different slices of the funnel.

Your Minnesota data is **neither** of these. It is not a police-recorded-crime count and not a victimization survey; it is a **court and sentencing** record, sitting below both of them in the funnel. So when you compare a finding from this data to a national crime statistic, first ask: *are we even measuring the same stage?* Usually you are not.

---

**References**

Bureau of Justice Statistics. (n.d.-b). *National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS).* U.S. Department of Justice. https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/ncvs

Federal Bureau of Investigation. (n.d.). *National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS).* U.S. Department of Justice. https://www.fbi.gov/how-we-can-help-you/more-fbi-services-and-information/ucr/nibrs

steps.title: One gentle look at the bottom of the funnel
steps.body:
Time for one short look.

Two things to notice:

1. The **count (N)** — the total number of rows. This is the bottom of the funnel made concrete: every case that survived every earlier stage across 2001–2019.
2. Soon you'll explore the **columns** available, over 100 of them. Each is an administrative field the Commission chose to record, and, just as importantly, a reminder of the many things it did *not* record.

Read the count, then answer the next question.

steps.title: How big is the bottom of the funnel?
steps.body: With no filters applied, what is the **count (N)**, the total number of sentencing records in the dataset?

steps.title: What is one row? The unit of analysis
steps.body:
A dataset is only as clear as its **unit of analysis**, what a single row represents. Here, one row is **one sentencing record**, and the Commission's counting rules define it precisely: when a person is sentenced in the same county for several offenses within a 30-day window, those are combined into a single record, described by the *most serious* offense (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2018).

Two consequences follow, and both are easy to get wrong:

- **One row is not one person.** Someone sentenced in 2004 and again in 2015 occupies *two* rows. The data counts sentencing events, not individuals the same distinction federal court statistics draw between a *case* and a *defendant* (Bureau of Justice Statistics, n.d.-a).
- **One row is not one crime.** Secondary offenses inside a single record are collapsed into the most serious one, and, as the funnel showed, most crimes never produce a record at all.

Whenever you read a total in this course, silently translate it: not "this many people," not "this many crimes," but **this many sentencing records**.

---

**References**

Bureau of Justice Statistics. (n.d.-a). *Federal criminal case processing statistics (FCCPS) tool: Supplementary information.* U.S. Department of Justice. https://bjs.ojp.gov/federal-criminal-case-processing-statistics-fccps-tool-supplementary-info

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2018). *Sentencing practices: Annual summary statistics for felony cases sentenced in 2017.* https://mn.gov/msgc-stat/documents/reports/2017/UpdatedMSGC2017AnnualSummaryStatistics.pdf

steps.title: Check: the unit of analysis
steps.body: The same individual is convicted of a felony and sentenced in 2006, and convicted of an unrelated felony and sentenced again in 2014. How many rows do they occupy in this dataset?

options: Two: each row is a sentencing record, not a person
One: the dataset tracks each unique person only once
It depends on whether the two crimes were similar

steps.title: What this dataset leaves out
steps.body:
Putting the funnel and the counting rules together, we can state plainly what these records **exclude**:

- **Unreported crime**: the dark figure, the majority of offenses (Biderman & Reiss, 1967).
- **Crimes that led to no felony conviction**: cases dropped, dismissed, acquitted, or resolved as misdemeanors.
- **Secondary offenses**: lesser charges folded into a record's most-serious charge (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2018).
- **Most white-collar and regulatory harm**: these offenses rarely produce a felony sentence, which by definition my result in a sentance of at least a year.
- **Context about people's lives**: the monitoring system does not maintain data on offenders' social or economic status (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2024).

One more caution belongs here: this is an *educational* dataset, not a prediction engine. It describes sentences that were imposed; it is not a calculator for what sentence any future case *should* or *will* receive. (The gap between the guideline-recommended sentence and what was actually pronounced is itself a subtle issue, we return to it in a later module.)

---

**References**

Biderman, A. D., & Reiss, A. J., Jr. (1967). On exploring the 'dark figure' of crime. *The Annals of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 374*(1), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1177/000271626737400102

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2018). *Sentencing practices: Annual summary statistics for felony cases sentenced in 2017.* https://mn.gov/msgc-stat/documents/reports/2017/UpdatedMSGC2017AnnualSummaryStatistics.pdf

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2024). *Report to the legislature.* https://www.lrl.mn.gov/docs/2024/mandated/240055.pdf

steps.title: Why a pattern in this data is not a verdict
steps.body:
Here is the interpretive habit this whole course is built to protect. A sentencing record is the end product of a long chain of human decisions by victims who report or don't, police who arrest or don't, prosecutors who charge or bargain, and judges who sentence. Every stage filters which cases arrive at the bottom (Klepper, Nagin, & Tierney, 1983). Studying only sentenced cases therefore means studying a **selected**, non-random sample of everything that happened (Bushway, Johnson, & Slocum, 2007).

So when you find that two groups have different average sentences, you have found a **description** of this data, not its cause. The common adage in statistics goes: corrolation does not equal causation. Furthermore, a landmark review of sentencing research concluded that even after accounting for the two strongest legal factors, offense severity and criminal history, most of the variation in sentences remains unexplained, and that observed group differences cannot by themselves be read as proof of discrimination (National Research Council, 1983).

Descriptive differences are real and worth finding. But *why* a difference exists is a separate, harder question, one a dedicated later module takes up with the care it demands.

---

**References**

Bushway, S., Johnson, B. D., & Slocum, L. A. (2007). Is the magic still there? The use of the Heckman two-step correction for selection bias in criminology. *Journal of Quantitative Criminology, 23*(2), 151–178. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10940-007-9024-4

Klepper, S., Nagin, D., & Tierney, L. J. (1983). Discrimination in the criminal justice system: A critical appraisal of the literature. In A. Blumstein, J. Cohen, S. E. Martin, & M. H. Tonry (Eds.), *Research on sentencing: The search for reform* (Vol. 2, pp. 55–128). National Academy Press.

National Research Council. (1983). *Research on sentencing: The search for reform* (A. Blumstein, J. Cohen, S. E. Martin, & M. H. Tonry, Eds.). National Academy Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/19436

steps.title: Check: description vs. explanation
steps.body: A cross-tabulation shows that offenses in Category A receive longer average presumptive sentences than offenses in Category B. Based on this module, which conclusion is justified?

options: There is a descriptive difference between the two groups in this data; the reasons behind it require further, careful analysis
The sentencing system is biased against Category A offenders
Category A crimes are inherently more harmful than Category B crimes

steps.title: The stakes: why careful reading matters
steps.body:
Why insist on all this caution? Because crime statistics are unusually easy to misuse, and the consequences are public. Despite a long-term decline in recorded violent crime since the early 1990s, the rate has fallen by roughly half, large majorities of Americans have repeatedly told pollsters that crime is getting worse (Gramlich, 2024). People reason confidently from numbers they have not examined, which is exactly the failure a data-literacy education is meant to prevent (Best, 2001).

You now hold the tools to do better. You can ask where a number sits in the funnel, what its unit of analysis is, and whether a pattern is being *described* or *explained*. Those three questions will carry you through every module that follows.

---

**References**

Best, J. (2001). *Damned lies and statistics: Untangling numbers from the media, politicians, and activists.* University of California Press.

Gramlich, J. (2024, April 24). *What the data says about crime in the U.S.* Pew Research Center. https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2024/04/24/what-the-data-says-about-crime-in-the-us/

steps.title: Put it in your own words
steps.body: In two or three sentences, name **one** question this dataset can answer well and **one** it cannot, and use the idea of the funnel to explain the difference.

answer.model_answer: A strong answer notes that the data can describe the sentences Minnesota courts imposed. For example, the typical presumptive sentence for a given offense category, because that is exactly the stage of the funnel the data records. It cannot measure how much crime occurred in Minnesota, because most crime never reaches sentencing: it drops out earlier in the funnel (unreported, unarrested, uncharged, or resolved below the felony level), and it drops out selectively rather than at random.

steps.title: Summary and full references
steps.body:
**The core ideas of this module:**

- This is **administrative data**, a by-product of running Minnesota's sentencing system, not a study designed to measure crime. The distinction is one between found and made data, thought is put into both kinds of data but the purpose of the consideration differs. For made data, which unlike the data you'll be working with, was collected specifically to address a hypothesis the question shaped the collection. Meanwhile data such as this sentencing commission dataset serves a functional purpose and thus is structured and collected with the needs of the system in mind. (Connelly et al., 2016).
- Furthermore, the data you'll be exploring sits at the **bottom of the funnel**: only cases that survived the gauntlet of every earlier stage in the criminal justice system and ended in a felony sentence appear (President's Commission on Law Enforcement and Administration of Justice, 1967).
- Most crime is in the **dark figure** and never enters at all, and not at random (Biderman & Reiss, 1967; Skogan, 1977).
- By **Sellin's dictum**, this makes the data an excellent record of *decisions* and a weak proxy for *crime* (Sellin, 1931).
- One row is **one sentencing record** not one person, not one crime (Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission, 2018).
- A pattern here is a **description**, not an explanation; causes require separate, careful analysis (National Research Council, 1983).

Carry three questions into every module that follows: *Where in the funnel does this number sit? What is its unit of analysis? Am I describing a pattern or explaining it?*

---

**References**

Best, J. (2001). *Damned lies and statistics: Untangling numbers from the media, politicians, and activists.* University of California Press.

Biderman, A. D., & Reiss, A. J., Jr. (1967). On exploring the 'dark figure' of crime. *The Annals of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 374*(1), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1177/000271626737400102

Bureau of Justice Statistics. (n.d.-a). *Federal criminal case processing statistics (FCCPS) tool: Supplementary information.* U.S. Department of Justice. https://bjs.ojp.gov/federal-criminal-case-processing-statistics-fccps-tool-supplementary-info

Bureau of Justice Statistics. (n.d.-b). *National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS).* U.S. Department of Justice. https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/ncvs

Bushway, S., Johnson, B. D., & Slocum, L. A. (2007). Is the magic still there? The use of the Heckman two-step correction for selection bias in criminology. *Journal of Quantitative Criminology, 23*(2), 151–178. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10940-007-9024-4

Connelly, R., Playford, C. J., Gayle, V., & Dibben, C. (2016). The role of administrative data in the big data revolution in social science research. *Social Science Research, 59*, 1–12. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssresearch.2016.04.015

Federal Bureau of Investigation. (n.d.). *National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS).* U.S. Department of Justice. https://www.fbi.gov/how-we-can-help-you/more-fbi-services-and-information/ucr/nibrs

Frase, R. S. (2005). Sentencing guidelines in Minnesota, 1978–2003. *Crime and Justice, 32*, 131–219. https://doi.org/10.1086/655354

Frase, R. S. (2013). *Just sentencing: Principles and procedures for a workable system.* Oxford University Press.

Gramlich, J. (2024, April 24). *What the data says about crime in the U.S.* Pew Research Center. https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2024/04/24/what-the-data-says-about-crime-in-the-us/

Klepper, S., Nagin, D., & Tierney, L. J. (1983). Discrimination in the criminal justice system: A critical appraisal of the literature. In A. Blumstein, J. Cohen, S. E. Martin, & M. H. Tonry (Eds.), *Research on sentencing: The search for reform* (Vol. 2, pp. 55–128). National Academy Press.

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (n.d.). *About MSGC.* https://mn.gov/sentencing-guidelines/about/

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2018). *Sentencing practices: Annual summary statistics for felony cases sentenced in 2017.* https://mn.gov/msgc-stat/documents/reports/2017/UpdatedMSGC2017AnnualSummaryStatistics.pdf

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2023). *2023 sentencing practices report: Summary statistics for felony cases.* https://mn.gov/sentencing-guidelines/assets/2023_MSGC_Annual_Summary_Statistics_tcm30-680133.pdf

Minnesota Sentencing Guidelines Commission. (2024). *Report to the legislature.* https://www.lrl.mn.gov/docs/2024/mandated/240055.pdf

Morgan, R. E., & Thompson, A. (2023). *Criminal victimization, 2022* (NCJ 307089). Bureau of Justice Statistics. https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/criminal-victimization-2022

National Research Council. (1983). *Research on sentencing: The search for reform* (A. Blumstein, J. Cohen, S. E. Martin, & M. H. Tonry, Eds.). National Academy Press. https://doi.org/10.17226/19436

President's Commission on Law Enforcement and Administration of Justice. (1967). *The challenge of crime in a free society.* U.S. Government Printing Office.

Sellin, T. (1931). The basis of a crime index. *Journal of Criminal Law and Criminology, 22*(3), 335–356.

Skogan, W. G. (1977). Dimensions of the dark figure of unreported crime. *Crime & Delinquency, 23*(1), 41–50. https://doi.org/10.1177/001112877702300104
